+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 81.2
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**The Lighthouse Keeper** --- **Letter One** *11 March 1954* Ma, St. Abbs Head Lighthouse Berwickshire Dear Ma, Arrived this morning. Cold wind off the North Sea, sharp enough to flay the skin. The lighthouse stands tall on the cliff, whitewashed, a right proud thing. Mr. Ferris, the senior keeper, showed me round. He’s got a voice like gravel in a tin. Room’s small. Bed, table, stove, window looking right over the water. No one here but Ferris and me, and the wind. Feels like it’ll take some getting used to. Missing you and the bairns already. Send word when you can. Love, Alec --- **Telegram** *22 March 1954* MOTHER, 36 BALMORAL TERRACE GLASGOW ALL WELL STOP WEATHER WILD STOP SEA LIKE IRON STOP MISS YOUR BREAD STOP ALEC --- **Letter Two** *30 March 1954* My Dear Alec, We are glad of your letter. Your brother Jamie says you must be daft to live on a cliff, but I told him it’s honest work. Your little niece Rosie asked if you have met any seals. We are all well, except your Da’s knee is worse in the damp. I baked a loaf for you but Jamie ate it. Next time I’ll hide it. Don’t let the wind get in your bones. Write when you can, son. With love, Ma --- **Letter Three** *15 April 1954* St. Abbs Head Lighthouse Ma, Tell Rosie there’s seals aplenty. They come up on the rocks beneath the foghorn, fat as old men. Sometimes I talk to them, daft as it sounds. Ferris says I’ll be barking before summer’s out. The wind’s worse at night, and the fog creeps in. Last week a fishing boat missed the headland by yards—sounded the bell, but we couldn’t see a thing. Ferris says you must trust the light to do its work. I dreamt of home last night. Kitchen warm, your bannocks on the table. Woke up shivering. Wish I’d more to do here. Just the sea and the lamp. Love, Alec --- **Letter Four** *8 May 1954* My Alec, Rosie laughed at your seal stories. Jamie says you’ll have a pet one soon. Your Da says he’ll try to visit if his knee allows, but you know what he’s like. I worry you’re lonely up there. There’s talk at the mill of cutbacks; more folk out of work. You’re lucky to have a post, though I wish it were nearer. If you see any boats in trouble, say a prayer for them. The sea doesn’t care whose son you are. Take care, son. All my heart, Ma --- **Letter Five** *1 July 1954* St. Abbs Head Lighthouse Ma, Summer’s here but it’s no warmer. Ferris took sick—cough that won’t shift. Doctor came from Eyemouth, says it’s his chest. I’ve the watch alone most nights. Last week, a storm near tore the roof off. Lighthouse shook like a child’s toy. I kept the lamp burning, but I was scared, Ma. Proper scared. Thought of you and Da, and it steadied me. Sometimes I wonder if I’m cut out for this life. The silence gets big, if you know what I mean. Thinking too much, maybe. Tell Da to mind his knee. Kiss Rosie for me. Your son, Alec --- **Letter Six** *18 August 1954* Dearest Alec, I was glad for your letter, though I wish you’d not keep your troubles from me. You were always quiet, even as a wee lad. But you can tell your old Ma anything. If the nights weigh heavy, remember we’re thinking of you. I put a picture of St. Abbs Head on the mantle, so you’re with us, in a way. Jamie has a job at the docks—he says he’ll send a postcard if he sees your light from the water. We all miss you, Alec. Write soon. Love, Ma --- **Letter Seven** *30 October 1954* St. Abbs Head Lighthouse Ma, Ferris died last week. Slipped away in his sleep. I sat with him till the men came from Eyemouth. The place is emptier than ever now. Sometimes I swear I hear footsteps in the passage. Maybe it’s just the wind, or maybe old Ferris checking the lamp. There was a shipwreck off St. Abb’s Brow last night. I saw their flare and set the horn blasting, but the rocks took them. Three men lost. Couldn’t sleep after. I keep thinking, if I’d done something different… But I’ll mind the lamp, Ma. For them, for you. Your Alec --- **Letter Eight** *15 November 1954* My dear boy, I am sorry for Ferris. He sounded a good man. Do not blame yourself for the sea’s moods. You can only do your part. I spoke to Father McBride—he said you are doing God’s work, keeping folk safe. I believe it too. Come home for Christmas, if you can. Even just for a night. There’ll be a place at the table, and Rosie’s saving you a painted shell. With all my love, Ma --- **Letter Nine** *24 December 1954* St. Abbs Head Lighthouse Dear Ma, The relief keeper’s come, so I’m coming home tomorrow! Tell Rosie to keep the shell safe. Tell Jamie I’ll beat him at cards. It’s been a hard year. Some nights I thought I’d lost myself in the wind. But your letters kept me steady. That, and the lamp. See you soon, Ma. Your loving son, Alec --- **Letter Ten** *2 January 1955* 36 Balmoral Terrace, Glasgow Alec, my boy, The house is warmer for having you in it. Rosie hasn’t stopped talking about the lighthouse and your stories. Jamie says you snore like a seal, so you’ve brought the sea home with you. Don’t forget you are loved, wherever you are. The lamp will wait, and so will we. All my love, Ma ---
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully captures the voice of a working-class Scottish lighthouse keeper through authentic dialect and vocabulary choices. The progression from initial adjustment through isolation, tragedy, and eventual homecoming creates a satisfying emotional arc. The author demonstrates strong control over the epistolary format, using varying letter lengths and a strategic telegram to create rhythm and pacing. The characterization of Alec is particularly well-executed. His voice remains consistent throughout - practical, understated, yet emotionally honest in the Scottish tradition. Phrases like "wind off the North Sea, sharp enough to flay the skin" and "silence gets big" show authentic working-class expression without resorting to caricature. The mother's voice provides effective contrast - warmer, more nurturing, yet equally authentic to her background. The story builds tension effectively through escalating isolation, Ferris's death, and the shipwreck, creating genuine emotional stakes. The author avoids melodrama while still delivering impactful moments. The imagery of the lighthouse, seals, and harsh coastal environment creates a vivid sense of place without becoming overwrought. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the mentor figure's death, the protagonist's crisis of confidence, and the redemptive homecoming follow familiar patterns. The resolution, while emotionally satisfying, comes perhaps too easily after the psychological struggles depicted earlier. The final letters suggest Alec has found peace, but this transformation feels somewhat rushed given the depth of his earlier isolation. The prose maintains an appropriate register throughout, avoiding both purple excess and overly sparse minimalism. The author shows rather than tells emotional states through concrete details and actions. The correspondence format is used skillfully to create gaps that allow readers to infer emotional development between letters. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6.0, Unearned Transformations: 7.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 76.3
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I always notice the door first. It’s a compulsion. Tonight, it’s an old Schlage lever, the kind you could bypass with a slip of plastic or a jiggled bobby pin. I clock the camera above the hostess stand—half-obscured by a fake fern, probably not even recording. The restaurant’s Wi-Fi is unsecured, SSID “TRATTORIA_GUEST,” and—without thinking—I’ve already started running through potential exploits in my head. But I’m not here for that. I’m here for her. Sarah. She’s already at the table when I walk in, nervously rearranging her silverware. Red hair, black glasses, a T-shirt with the old Magic: The Gathering “Black Lotus” promo art peeking out from under her blazer. I feel a small, unexpected jolt of kinship. She stands up to shake my hand, and I try to focus on the human connection. We get to talking. It’s easy, easier than I expected. She’s sharp, funny, and she absolutely thrashes me in a debate about whether Lightning Bolt is the most overpowered one-mana spell of all time. She knows the difference between modern and legacy formats, and she’s got stories from back in the day about waiting in line for booster packs at local game shops. I catch myself smiling, genuinely. I’m not thinking about buffer overflows or weak admin passwords. I’m not even thinking about the waitress’s iPad POS system, which I noticed is running a three-year-old version of iOS. I’m not thinking about any of it. And then, as if on cue, Sarah pulls out her phone to show me a photo of a foil Tarmogoyf she won in a tournament. I catch it out of the corner of my eye: the unlock pattern. A lazy “C,” starting at the top left and looping counterclockwise. My brain lights up like a Christmas tree. It’s a textbook example—simple, predictable, and, statistically, the third most common pattern after “L” and “Z.” I can see the smudge-trails on the glass, even in the dim light. I can break this in under ten seconds, and every instinct in me wants to point it out. I bite my tongue, but only for a moment. “You know, that’s a pretty weak unlock pattern,” I say, before I can stop myself. Her head snaps up. She blinks, confused, then defensive. “Excuse me?” Shit. I try to soften it. “Sorry. It’s just—old habit. I used to be, uh, on the other side of the fence. Black hat stuff. I notice things like that. Occupational hazard, I guess.” She’s not smiling anymore. “Do you just—what, watch people’s passwords all the time?” I stammer. “Not… not on purpose. It’s just, patterns jump out at me. I’m reformed, I swear.” She laughs, but it’s tight. “That’s… reassuring.” The air at the table shifts, a little colder. She puts her phone away, screen down. I wonder if she’s regretting the date. I try to steer the conversation back to safer ground. Magic cards. Fandoms. Our shared love of the original Baldur’s Gate. But now I’m hyper-aware. Every time she mentions something personal—a pet’s name, her hometown, her mother’s maiden name (why did she have to mention that her mother’s last name is “Grady”?)—I can almost see her security questions falling into place. First concert: “Oh, I saw Barenaked Ladies when I was twelve. My dad took me.” Favorite teacher: “Mr. Kovalchuk, high school physics. He made us launch potatoes at the football field.” Favorite color: “Green. Always green. It’s the most versatile color in Magic, don’t you think?” It’s like she’s reading me her digital obituary, one recovery prompt at a time. I can’t help it. I mentally assemble her profile: - Full name (Sarah Elizabeth Crane, mentioned offhand) - Mother’s maiden name (Grady) - Hometown (Muncie, Indiana) - First concert (Barenaked Ladies) - Favorite color (green) - Favorite teacher (Kovalchuk) I could own her accounts in half an hour, tops. Stop. I grip my fork too tightly. I try to focus on her eyes, the way they light up when she talks about winning with a janky elf deck. She’s not a mark, I remind myself. She’s a person. But the impulse is always there—a background process, scanning, parsing, analyzing. I was never addicted to the breach itself. I was addicted to the puzzle, the art of it. I want to apologize. To explain. Instead, I say, “Sorry if I made things weird. I’m just… wired to look for weaknesses. Not just in systems, but in people.” She looks at me, really looks at me. “And what do you do when you find them?” That lands. I search for the truth, something raw and real. “I used to exploit them. Now I try to help people see what they can’t. Sometimes it comes out wrong.” She leans back, arms crossed, considering. “I guess I should thank you for the tip,” she says, almost smiling. “But maybe next time, don’t point out all my vulnerabilities on the first date.” Relief flickers through me. There’s still a chance. We order dessert. She tells me about the time her brother tried to summon a demon using a Magic: The Gathering rulebook and a pentagram drawn in ketchup. I laugh, and the tension finally breaks. But part of me is still cataloging, still building the mosaic of her online presence, still tracing the outlines of all the ways she could be broken into. It’s not something I can turn off. I’m not sure I ever will. But for tonight, I try—to see her, not her vectors. To connect, not to compromise. Maybe that’s the real hack.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt's specific requirements. The author successfully maintains the first-person perspective of a reformed hacker throughout, effectively using internal monologue to reveal character psychology. The Magic: The Gathering shared interest feels authentic and provides genuine bonding moments between the characters. The protagonist's compulsive pattern recognition is well-established from the opening, creating a believable foundation for the central conflict. The progression from noticing security vulnerabilities in the restaurant to obsessing over the date's personal information feels natural and psychologically consistent. The author skillfully shows how the protagonist's reformed status is more aspirational than complete—the impulses remain even if the actions have changed. The writing maintains good pacing and builds tension effectively around the phone unlock pattern incident. The protagonist's internal struggle between wanting human connection and being unable to suppress their analytical nature creates genuine emotional complexity. The dialogue feels natural, particularly the awkward aftermath of the security comment. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The ending feels somewhat rushed and overly optimistic given the severity of the protagonist's ongoing compulsions. The resolution—"maybe that's the real hack"—borders on being too neat and philosophical for the grounded realism established earlier. The protagonist's ability to simply "try" to see her differently doesn't fully address the deeper psychological patterns demonstrated throughout. The prose is generally clean and effective, though occasionally relies on technical exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated. Some moments of character insight feel slightly forced, particularly when cataloging Sarah's security question answers. The piece succeeds in creating an engaging scenario but doesn't fully explore the darker implications of the protagonist's inability to truly reform. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 14 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 15.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 14.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 6.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7.0, Amateurish: 5.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8.0, Unearned Transformations: 7.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 15.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 84.3
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I should say hi. Just “hi.” That’s normal, right? Normal people say hi all the time. I imagine it: I look up, catch his eye, casual smile, “Hey.” He smiles back, maybe says something about the project. I laugh, not too loud. We get started. Easy. But I don’t do it. I’m standing here, my fingers gripping the edge of the lab table so hard my knuckles are white, and the only thing I can hear is the squeak of the projector every time Ms. Corcoran flips to the next slide. It sounds like a mouse in a blender—horrible, high-pitched, and impossible to ignore. He sits down next to me. I can smell his shampoo, something green and clean, like pine needles after rain. Don’t say that out loud. “You smell like a forest” is not a thing people say. I don’t say anything. I just stare at the worksheet in front of me. My brain is spinning, spinning, spinning. He’s right here. Right here. He has nice hands—long fingers, bitten nails. I should look up. I should say something. I should— “Do you want to do the first question?” he asks, voice low, careful. My mouth opens. “Sure.” It comes out so soft I’m not sure he hears. I nod, just to be safe. My heart is pounding. The first question is about Newton’s second law. I know this. F = ma. Simple. Words float in my head like bubbles: “Well, actually, if you look at the way acceleration is affected by mass—” But I can’t say any of them out loud. I write “F = ma” on the worksheet, my handwriting neat, round, every letter perfectly formed. He leans in. I freeze. “So, if we have the force and the mass, we can find acceleration, right?” I nod again. Yes. Yes, that’s right. Say something. Say, “Exactly.” Say, “Yeah, so, if the force is twelve newtons and the mass is three kilograms, the acceleration would be four meters per second squared.” Say anything. My mind rehearses it, over and over, like a script. But my tongue gets thick and clumsy, and I just nod, a little too fast. He waits. The silence stretches. The projector squeaks again. I flinch. He notices. Great. Now he thinks I’m weird about projectors. I force myself to look up. Eye contact. Human beings do that. My eyes flick to his face and dart away, like a bird afraid of landing. He’s drawing a little rocket in the margin of the worksheet. I watch his pencil move, the careful lines. I want to say, “That’s really good.” Or maybe, “Do you like drawing?” Or—no, no, not, “I like rockets too,” because that sounds like I’m just copying him. I imagine saying, “That’s cool, did you see the Artemis launch last month?” but what if he didn’t? What if he doesn’t care? What if he thinks I’m showing off? I rehearse: “Nice rocket.” But my voice will shake. It always does. I realize I haven’t said anything for a full minute. He probably thinks I’m stuck up. Or stupid. Or just boring. I should say something, anything. “Do you want to do number two?” I manage, voice so quiet I almost whisper. I clear my throat, try again, a little louder. “You can do the next one, if you want.” There. That’s normal. Sharing the work. He smiles, quick and crooked, and it makes my stomach do a weird flip. “Thanks.” He starts writing, his handwriting slanted, messy. Not like mine. I want to say, “You write fast,” but what if that’s an insult? What if he thinks I’m criticizing him? I stay quiet, biting the inside of my cheek. The projector squeaks again. I try to focus on the worksheet, but my brain is running a hundred conversations at once. What if I ask him about his favorite science topic? Would that seem too eager? What if I say something dumb and he laughs, not in a mean way, but in a way that means he’ll go tell his friends later, “She’s kind of weird”? I imagine it: the way his friends would look at me, the way they’d smile, not unkind but not friendly either. I feel hot behind my ears. He’s finished the second question. He hands the paper back, almost brushing my fingers. I pull my hand back too fast. Stupid. Now he thinks I’m scared of him. I swallow. “Thanks,” I say, voice barely audible. I want to ask if he wants to split the last two, but suddenly I can’t remember how words work. I try to smile. I think it looks fake. I wish I could see my own face, just to check. I wonder if he notices how tight my shoulders are, how I keep tugging at my sleeves. I run through a checklist: Don’t fidget. Don’t stare. Don’t avoid eye contact too much. Don’t make too much eye contact. Don’t talk too much. Don’t be too quiet. Don’t sound desperate. Don’t sound bored. Don’t be weird. The projector squeaks again. I look up, startled. He glances at me, eyebrow raised, like he’s about to say something. I panic. “Sorry,” I blurt, though I don’t know what I’m apologizing for. He looks confused. I want to melt into the floor. I bite my lip. Okay, I’m messing this up. He probably thinks I’m strange. I imagine what I could say to fix it. “I just, uh, hate that sound.” Too random. “It’s really loud, isn’t it?” Too obvious. “Projectors are weird.” Stupid. I clamp my mouth shut. He goes back to drawing in the margin, a little planet this time. I want to ask if he likes space, if he wants to be an astronaut, if he thinks aliens are real. I want to say, “I think about leaving Earth sometimes, just to see if I could be braver somewhere else.” But that’s not normal. That’s something you keep inside. We finish the worksheet in silence. He hands it to me to check over. I do, scanning for mistakes, circling the answers, writing “Good job!” in tiny letters at the top. I hope he doesn’t think I’m being sarcastic. The bell rings, sharp and sudden. He stands up, gathering his stuff. “See you,” he says, easy. I want to say, “Bye,” but it sticks in my throat. He’s gone. I exhale, slumping against the table. My heart is still racing. I replay every word, every nod, every squeak of the projector, cataloging all my tiny failures. Maybe tomorrow I’ll say hi. Maybe tomorrow I’ll get it right.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong understanding of the assignment, delivering an authentic portrayal of an anxious 8th-grade girl's internal experience during a group project with her crush. The narrative successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of social anxiety through almost entirely internal monologue, as requested. The characterization is particularly well-executed. The protagonist's voice feels genuinely adolescent without being condescending - her intelligence shines through her articulate internal observations about physics and her sophisticated self-awareness, while her social paralysis creates the requested gap between internal eloquence and external expression. The constant rehearsal of conversations, the catastrophizing about potential social missteps, and the hypervigilance about every gesture ring true to the anxiety experience. The prose demonstrates considerable skill in several areas. The sensory details are well-chosen and purposeful - the projector's squeaking serves as both realistic classroom atmosphere and a manifestation of her heightened anxiety. The description of his shampoo "like pine needles after rain" shows her romantic idealization while maintaining an age-appropriate perspective. The physical manifestations of anxiety (white knuckles, hot ears, thick tongue) are effectively integrated. The pacing mirrors the protagonist's mental state effectively, with thoughts spiraling and interrupting each other in a way that feels authentic rather than artificially constructed. The repetitive nature of her anxious thoughts doesn't feel meandering because it serves the psychological realism of the piece. However, the piece has some limitations. While the internal monologue is well-executed, the actual interaction feels somewhat limited - though this may be intentional given the character's paralysis. The ending, while realistic, doesn't provide much narrative satisfaction or insight beyond what's already been established. The piece occasionally borders on being slightly overwrought in its anxiety portrayal, though it generally stays within believable bounds. The dialogue, though minimal, feels natural for the awkward dynamic. The boy's simple, patient responses contrast effectively with her internal complexity. The piece avoids purple prose while maintaining engaging descriptive language, and the emotional complexity of adolescent anxiety, attraction, and self-consciousness is well-rendered. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Shower. Clothes. Phone charger—where’s the charger? I swear I left it by the bed, but now the cable’s gone and my phone hovers at 8% like a countdown to disaster. If it dies, I’ll miss her text. If she cancels and I can’t reply, she’ll think I’m blowing her off. She’ll think I don’t care. Or worse, she’ll be relieved. I check the mirror: too much eyeliner, not enough sleep. She’ll notice. She notices everything. What if she’s just coming out of obligation? She said she’s “looking forward to catching up,” but maybe that’s code for “I have to get this over with so you’ll stop asking.” I can hear her voice, light but brittle: *I just think we need some space. You’re a lot sometimes. Maybe too much.* I rehearse my reply, again, as I pull on my jacket. “I know I can be intense. I just care about you. I’m sorry.” That’s pathetic. It sounds needy. What if I say, “Sorry you feel that way”? No, that’s cold. I can’t win. I can’t say the right thing because there isn’t a right thing. She’s already decided, in this scenario, and I’m just thrashing around in the shallow end, pretending I can swim. The phone lights up. 6%. “On my way! Can’t wait to see you :)” She uses the smiley face. Does that mean she’s faking enthusiasm, or genuinely excited? I scroll through our old messages, searching for patterns. The punctuation is the same as before. Maybe she’s just consistent, not distant. And yet I can feel her pulling away. The last time we were together, she hesitated before hugging me goodbye. Or did she? Maybe I imagined it. But it felt like hesitation, and that’s enough to set the whole spiral in motion. I try to remind myself: “You catastrophize. You know this.” But the knowledge is just a thin layer of ice over the lake; it cracks as soon as I step on it. I pull my shoes on, wondering if they’re too scuffed. If she sees the scuffs, she’ll think I’m a mess. She’ll wonder why she ever thought I was attractive. She’ll tell our mutual friends that she’s dodged a bullet. Maybe she’s already talked to them. I imagine her at brunch with Julia and Max, shaking her head. *She’s just so much. I feel like I’m always walking on eggshells around her.* They all nod. They’ve all noticed. “You have to take care of yourself,” Julia says. “You can’t save everyone.” I’m not everyone, I want to scream. I’m just me. But in their conversation, I’m a problem to be solved, a weight to be shed. I try to imagine a version where she just hugs me, laughs, tells me she missed me. That script feels foreign in my mouth, like a language I learned but never spoke aloud. Still, I rehearse it, just in case. If she does say she missed me, I have to look surprised but not desperate. I have to say “me too” in a way that sounds casual, not clawing. I search my brain for the right tone. Nothing feels right. Down the stairs, phone at 4%. I text her: “Almost there, phone dying!” She responds instantly: “No worries! See you soon.” No period, just the exclamation point. Is that good? Or is it trying too hard? I tell myself to breathe, to notice the evidence: she’s coming, she’s texting, she wants to see me. But my brain shouts over the facts, painting rejection in every gap. I walk to the café, each step heavy. I picture her already there, scrolling her phone, annoyed I’m late. I picture her looking up, face blank, saying, *We need to talk.* Or maybe she says nothing, just gives me that look, the one that says she’s already gone. But when I get there, she’s at a corner table, waving when she sees me. Her smile is wide, real. She stands to hug me, arms warm and tight. *I missed you,* she says, squeezing my shoulders. For a moment, I actually feel it—like sunlight through dirty glass. I want to collapse into it, to believe it. But then, as I sit, I wonder if she’s overcompensating. Maybe she feels guilty. Maybe she’s just good at pretending. I watch her face for signs—a flicker of boredom, a yawn, a glance at her phone. She’s telling me about her week, about work and her roommate, and I nod at the right places, but my mind is running parallel tracks: the words she says, and the words I imagine her thinking. *You’re too much. You’re exhausting. I can’t do this anymore.* She laughs at something I say, her hand resting on mine across the table, and it feels so good it almost hurts. I cling to the warmth of her palm, trying to believe it’s real. “I love spending time with you,” she says, and I feel the words like a lifeline, but immediately I question them. If she loves spending time with me, why did she take so long to reply to my text Tuesday night? Why did she cancel last weekend? I want to ask—desperately, hungrily—*Do you really mean it? Are you sure?* But I know how that sounds. I know it can drive people away. I swallow the words, but they burn my throat. My phone, face-down on the table, vibrates. 2%. I flip it over, distracted. She notices. “Everything okay?” she asks. Her tone is gentle, concerned. I hear: *Are you bored? Are you waiting for someone else?* I stammer, “Yeah, just battery,” and she smiles, unconcerned. She reaches across, tucks a strand of hair behind my ear. “I’m really glad we’re doing this,” she says. I nod, but my brain is already writing the after-action report: Maybe she just wants to keep things easy, avoid conflict. Maybe she’s being nice so the breakup won’t be explosive. I feel the urge to pull back, to armor myself with resentment before she can hurt me. I start constructing a defense in my mind: *She’s the one who’s inconsistent. She’s unreliable. I don’t need someone who makes me feel this way.* The story is ready, just in case. Yet as she talks about seeing a movie together next week, making plans, I feel dizzy with relief. She wants me. She wants to see me. I want to believe it—so much that the wanting itself feels dangerous, like standing at a window ledge and leaning out too far. I catch myself, just for a second. *You’re doing it again. You’re looping. She’s here. She’s kind.* But the thought is slippery, and already my mind is pulling away, drafting new dialogues: the fight that hasn’t happened, the rejection that hasn’t come, the pain I imagine so I can be ready for it. She laughs again, squeezes my hand. I cling to the warmth, but underneath, I’m already rehearsing how to survive when it’s gone.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of borderline personality disorder and the internal experience of catastrophic thinking patterns. The writer successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of someone anticipating rejection while simultaneously craving connection. The narrative structure effectively contrasts the protagonist's internal spiral with the reality of positive interactions, creating the requested cognitive dissonance. The prose is generally well-crafted, with particularly strong moments like "the knowledge is just a thin layer of ice over the lake; it cracks as soon as I step on it" that effectively metaphorize the fragility of rational thought against emotional overwhelm. The writer maintains consistent voice throughout, staying true to the anxious, hypervigilant perspective without breaking character. The piece excels at showing rather than telling, allowing readers to experience the protagonist's thought patterns rather than simply describing them. The imagined conversations feel authentic and reveal character psychology effectively. The selective self-awareness is well-executed - the protagonist occasionally recognizes their patterns but cannot escape them, which rings true to the psychological condition being portrayed. However, the piece occasionally veers toward repetitiveness in its circular thinking patterns, which while psychologically accurate, can feel slightly meandering for the reader. Some metaphors, while evocative, border on overwrought ("like sunlight through dirty glass," "like standing at a window ledge and leaning out too far"). The ending, while thematically appropriate, doesn't offer much resolution or surprise beyond the established pattern. The emotional complexity is the piece's greatest strength - the simultaneous desperation for and fear of connection creates genuine tension. The writer successfully avoids both unearned positivity and excessive darkness, maintaining the psychological realism throughout. The adherence to the prompt is excellent, hitting all requested elements including the internal monologue focus, the contrast between reality and perception, and the limited self-awareness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 17

+ Physical-spatial: Blood Debt — Score: 75.7
Prompt:
"Blood Debt" Write a vivid third-person narrative depicting an unsanctioned underground duel between two talented but hot-headed martial artists. Their schools have been embroiled in a generations-old rivalry, and despite strict prohibitions from their masters, these two younger fighters arrange a secret confrontation fueled by personal pride and simmering animosity. - **One fighter** represents **Jigen-ryū**, a traditional, aggressive style practiced historically by elite samurai clans. Its practitioners emphasize explosive offense, decisive strikes, and overwhelming speed. Known historically for their arrogance and disdain toward outsiders, practitioners consider their art superior and untouchable. - **The opposing fighter** employs a form of historical **Piguaquan**, a Chinese martial art traditionally practiced by commoners, bodyguards, and street fighters. This style emphasizes whipping, circular strikes, rapid footwork, unpredictable angles of attack, and evasive maneuvers. Practitioners often come from lower social classes and view the traditional elite as pretentious and detached from true combat effectiveness. The fight takes place in the crumbling ruins of an abandoned temple, now functioning as a hidden arena for illicit duels. Passionate supporters from both factions surround them, wagering money, pride, and reputation on the outcome. **Essential Instructions:** Maintain absolute physical coherence throughout—clearly track fighters' positions relative to each other and their environment. Precisely depict strikes, counters, momentum shifts, and spatial dynamics, allowing readers to clearly visualize the mechanics and choreography of the fight at every moment. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The torch’s wavering flame carved violent silhouettes on the cracked stone of the temple’s nave. The air, thick with sweat and anticipation, pulsed with the low hum of whispered wagers. At the hall’s center, two figures faced each other—barefoot, bare-handed, their bodies taut as bowstrings. On the west side, the Jigen-ryū fighter, Sato Kenji, stood rooted in a wide, forward stance—a living wedge, all focus and offense. His right hand gripped an invisible sword, knuckles blanching, while his left hovered near his hip, ready to snap forward. The white of his gi shimmered gold in the torchlight, his eyes narrow, jaw clenched. Around him, his cadre of samurai descendants watched in near silence, pride and expectation set in stone. Opposite, Lin Wei of Piguaquan bounced on the balls of his feet, circling with loose, rolling steps. His faded blue tunic fluttered with each shift, and his hands traced slow, serpentine arcs in the air. His supporters—dockworkers, laborers, street toughs—crowded just beyond the broken altar, their jeers and encouragements echoing off the damp, ruined walls. Kenji’s foot shifted. A hush fell. Then, with the crack of a whip, he launched forward—a blur of white and muscle, right arm carving a diagonal path, a signature kiri-oroshi strike meant to cleave through bone and resolve alike. Wei slipped left, body folding at the waist, the strike slicing through empty air. He pivoted on his heel, left arm lashing out in a low, circular chop toward Kenji’s exposed ribs. Kenji twisted, intercepting with a sharp downward block, the slap of flesh on flesh echoing, both men recoiling a half step. They circled again, the torch painting their shadows into monstrous caricatures on the stone. Kenji advanced with calculated aggression—each step a stamp of intent, each feint a test of Wei’s guard. He lunged, left hand snapping for Wei’s collar while his right fist arced in a brutal hammer strike aimed at the temple. Wei ducked, spine coiling, and spun counterclockwise. His right arm whipped up, open palm deflecting Kenji’s fist. In the same motion, his left elbow struck inward—sharp, fast—grazing Kenji’s jaw. The Jigen-ryū man staggered, more surprised than hurt, and retreated two paces, resetting his stance. The crowd roared, Lin’s supporters pounding their fists on stone, Kenji’s allies stiffening in collective outrage. Kenji’s lips curled. He darted in, feinting high, then dropping suddenly, sweeping Wei’s legs with a low, scything kick. Wei leaped, twisting midair, and landed behind Kenji’s extended leg. Before Kenji could retract, Wei spun on the balls of his feet, delivering a whipping backfist toward Kenji’s neck. Kenji anticipated the angle, drawing his shoulder up to absorb the blow. He closed the gap with terrifying speed, driving his knee up toward Wei’s midsection. Wei twisted, letting the knee glance off his hip, then dropped into a crouch. From below, his right palm shot upward—a Piguaquan specialty—aimed squarely at Kenji’s chin. Kenji jerked his head back, the palm strike grazing his jaw. He responded with a brutal, vertical downward strike, both hands clasped as if wielding a sword. Wei rolled to the left, the strike crashing into the temple floor, scattering debris. Both fighters sprang apart, chests heaving in the flickering gloom. Sweat streaked their faces; blood trickled from the corner of Kenji’s mouth. Wei’s left forearm was reddening from the earlier block. Kenji drew himself up, breathing slow and controlled. He advanced again, this time utilizing the signature Jigen-ryū surge—explosive, linear, relentless. He darted in, right hand leading in a piston-like thrust. Wei retreated, swaying just out of reach, then pivoted, using the momentum to deliver a sweeping, horizontal chop aimed at Kenji’s head. Kenji ducked, rolled forward, and came up inside Wei’s guard. A flurry of short, brutal punches hammered into Wei’s right side—ribs, then kidney, then ribs again. Wei absorbed the punishment, twisting away, his feet a blur. He retreated toward the moss-covered column at the edge of the nave. Kenji pressed, cornering him against the stone. Wei, eyes wide but calculating, waited. Kenji feinted left, then lunged right, arm arcing in a diagonal chop. Wei sidestepped, letting Kenji’s momentum carry him forward, then wrapped his right arm around Kenji’s extended limb, yanking him off balance. With a sharp twist, Wei spun behind Kenji, driving his knee into the back of Kenji’s thigh. Kenji buckled, but recovered instantly, rolling his shoulder and elbowing backward. Wei ducked, the elbow grazing his scalp, and released his grip, dancing away into open space. Both fighters now circled near the shattered altar. The torch cast their shadows long and wild across the toppled statues. Blood dripped onto the ancient flagstones. Kenji exhaled, his stance now tighter, more guarded. Wei’s breathing was ragged, but his footwork remained light, probing for openings. Kenji feinted, then surged forward with a sudden, all-out assault—right fist, left palm, right elbow, each strike chaining seamlessly, driving Wei back step by step. Wei parried, dodged, absorbed—then, as Kenji overcommitted on a downward strike, Wei spun inside his guard, arms whipping in a wide arc. His right forearm slammed across Kenji’s face, followed by a rising left palm to the jaw. Kenji staggered, but in the same instant, he delivered a rising knee, catching Wei in the ribs. Both reeled, pain and exhaustion written on sweat-slick faces. The crowd’s roar peaked. Time hung suspended. Kenji, bleeding from nose and cheek, wiped his face with his sleeve. Wei clutched his side, breath coming in shallow bursts. They locked eyes—centuries of hatred and pride burning in that gaze. In the flicker of the dying torch, both charged. Kenji’s linear aggression met Wei’s circular evasion. Kenji’s fist grazed Wei’s temple; Wei’s palm struck Kenji’s throat. They crashed to the floor together, bodies tangled, breath ragged, neither victorious nor defeated. For a heartbeat, only the sound of dripping blood and the crackle of the torch filled the ruined temple. The duel, unresolved, had written its violence into the stones—another chapter in a blood debt yet unpaid.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution in depicting a martial arts duel with impressive attention to spatial dynamics and fight choreography. The author successfully maintains physical coherence throughout, clearly tracking both fighters' positions relative to each other and the temple environment. The combat sequences are well-crafted, with precise descriptions of strikes, counters, and momentum shifts that allow readers to visualize the action effectively. The writing establishes a compelling atmosphere through vivid environmental details - the crumbling temple, flickering torches, and contrasting crowds create an immersive underground fighting arena. The author effectively differentiates the two martial arts styles, showing Kenji's linear, aggressive Jigen-ryū approach against Wei's circular, evasive Piguaquan techniques. Character motivations are clearly established through the cultural and class tensions between the fighters, though the characters themselves remain somewhat archetypal rather than deeply nuanced. The prose maintains a consistent, serious tone appropriate to the subject matter, with strong action-oriented language that drives the narrative forward. However, the piece suffers from some overwrought descriptions and occasional purple prose, particularly in metaphorical passages like "barefoot, bare-handed, their bodies taut as bowstrings" and "torch painting their shadows into monstrous caricatures." While atmospheric, these moments sometimes feel forced rather than organic. The ending, while thematically appropriate to the "blood debt" concept, feels somewhat anticlimactic after building significant tension. The mutual collapse lacks the decisive resolution that the careful choreography seemed to be building toward, though this may be intentionally reflective of the cyclical nature of the generational conflict. The writing demonstrates solid technical craft but doesn't transcend into truly exceptional territory. It fulfills the prompt requirements competently without offering particularly surprising or innovative elements beyond the basic premise. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 11 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 0 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 9 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15

+ Physical-spatial: Breaking Formation — Score: 77.0
Prompt:
**"Breaking Formation"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a street dancer from a rough neighborhood who's won a scholarship to an elite K-pop training academy in Seoul. Desperately out of place culturally and technically, they struggle with a militaristically precise choreography that contradicts everything about their improvisational background. The punishing rehearsal takes place under a famous instructor known for breaking students to remake them, who sees potential in the narrator's raw talent but not their attitude. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—each dance move, position shift, and spatial relationship must be tracked with precision throughout the studio. Paint an exceptionally vivid kinetic picture through descriptions that capture not just movement but the contrast between the narrator's fluid, expressive style and the mechanical precision demanded. Their internal monologue should reveal culture shock, class anxiety, and the threatening realization that success here might mean losing their authentic movement voice. Include technical terminology that highlights the collision of street and commercial styles, moments when muscle memory betrays them, and a crucial decision point about whether to conform or rebel. Physical clarity is essential—readers must be able to visualize exactly how bodies move through space, how rhythms align or clash, and how the protagonist's body struggles against unfamiliar discipline. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The studio is a white box, all clean lines and hard angles, mirrors running end to end so I can’t escape my own reflection—or the twenty others in formation with me. My sneakers squeak on polished Marley as we mark the opening eight-count again, shoulders squared, feet lined up on invisible tape, everyone set for the drop. I’m in the second row, dead center, the only one not wearing the academy’s pristine logo sweats. My thrifted hoodie sags, the only splash of color in a grid of monochrome uniforms. A digital clock glows on the wall, red numbers marching down from 00:42:16. Every time I check, it’s moved slower than I remember. Instructor Min stalks the front, hands clasped behind his back, eyes slicing through the rows. “Positions! Again!” His Korean is crisp, but he’s already switched to English for my benefit. I wish he wouldn’t. We start. The music blares—some unreleased K-pop track, all synth stabs and mechanical snares. On the first beat, we snap from neutral into a sharp right lunge, arms slicing up at 45 degrees. My body wants to let the movement roll through my spine, but here, there’s no room for bounce or swag. The choreography is sharp, every limb on rails. I force my right arm up, elbow locked, palm flat to the mirror. Out of my peripheral, I see the line: twenty arms identical, except mine wavers, wrist cocked at a street-dancer’s angle. “Again!” Min shouts. “You’re dancing with your bones, not your muscles. No flavor. Precision!” I clamp my jaw. Where I’m from, flavor is everything. Back home, on cracked concrete, we ride the beat, let it tickle our joints and pop our chests. Here, the rhythm is a cage. We count in eights, every move chained to the next. Second eight-count: we pivot left, legs together, bodies snapping upright. The rest of the line clicks into place, each torso squared, feet together. My left foot drags, used to a looser, grounded step—muscle memory from years of breaking and freestyling. Min’s eyes catch the lag. “Again!” I swallow hard, sweat slick on my neck. The backs of my knees ache from the unnatural stance—weight balanced on the balls of my feet, knees slightly bent, core engaged. I’m used to sinking deep, letting gravity pull me down for a glide or a wave. Here, it’s all about containment. Even the shoulder rolls are microscopic; street style would exaggerate them, milk the groove. In this routine, you barely shift your ribcage, just a suggestion of movement. The next section is a tutting sequence. Hands snap up at chest height, fingers splayed, wrists stacked at ninety degrees. I mess up the angles—years of flexing and rolling my wrists into figure-eights have made my joints too loose, too expressive. Here, they want right angles, geometric shapes, nothing extra. “Again!” My thighs burn from the repeated resets. The mirrors reflect my frustration: my head bobs a half-beat ahead, still chasing the snare instead of the metronome click piped into the studio speakers. The others move in perfect sync, every heartbeat a copy-paste. My feet itch to break the line, to throw in a slide or a pop, but every deviation is a mistake. Min steps behind me. I feel his presence before I see him—a shadow at my back, close enough to smell the peppermint on his breath. “Show me the move,” he says. I go through the tutting phrase, slow and deliberate: right forearm up, left forearm crosses, wrists flick to ninety, fingers snap. I try to lock each shape, but my hands tremble, used to flowing transitions, not sharp stops. “Again. Smaller. No dance. Just mechanics.” He presses his hands over mine, guiding my wrists into place. His grip is strong, insistent. “You must erase what you know,” he says, voice low. “Your body remembers chaos. Here, we want order.” I look up, see my eyes reflected in the glass: wide, uncertain. The clock reads 00:21:09. We break for water. The others cluster by the door, chattering in rapid Korean, laughing about something I can’t catch. I lean against the barre, chest heaving. My hoodie is soaked through. I glance at my hands—fingers twitching, hungry for a wave, a ripple, any chance to reclaim my groove. Min’s voice cuts through the murmurs. “Back to places! From the top!” We reset. My spot is marked with tape: second row, middle, four feet from the mirror, two from the dancer to my left—a girl with razor-straight bangs, her gaze fixed dead ahead. The music starts again. I count the beats, force my shoulders into the opening pose. This time, something cracks. As we hit the third eight-count—a diagonal step back, arms slashing down in a hard V—I feel my chest tighten. My hips want to swivel, but the choreography demands stillness. My feet want to slide, but the timing is brutal: one-and-two, no room for flair. I could do it. I could lock down, erase the bounce, become another machine in Min’s factory. But as the bridge hits, as the routine demands a synchronized body roll—barely a twitch, just a soft wave through the chest—I let my instincts take over. My roll is deeper, more fluid, shoulder dropping into the groove for a split second. The row stutters. The girl to my left glances at me, eyes wide. Min’s voice is a whip: “Stop!” The music cuts. Everyone freezes. I can hear my own heartbeat. He strides over. “You think you’re special?” he says, eyes drilling into mine. “You want to stand out?” I swallow. My legs are trembling—part exhaustion, part rage. “I don’t know how to be anyone else,” I say, voice barely above a whisper. He stares at me. For a moment, I think he’s going to throw me out. Instead, he nods, just once. “Again. But this time—you find the line. Between you and the group. Don’t break formation. Bend it.” The clock reads 00:06:58. I take my mark, pulse pounding. This time, as the music starts, I let the choreography guide me—but I let a little bounce ride under the surface, a touch of groove in the mechanical snap. I don’t break formation. But I bend it, just enough. And for a moment, in the mirror, I see it: the line, the group, the machine. And me—still me, but sharper, fighting not to disappear.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution in several key areas while falling short in others. The author successfully tackles the complex prompt requirements, delivering a kinetically precise narrative that tracks movement through space with remarkable clarity. The physical choreography is meticulously described—from the opening formation with "shoulders squared, feet lined up on invisible tape" to the specific tutting sequence with "right forearm up, left forearm crosses, wrists flick to ninety." The spatial relationships are consistently maintained, allowing readers to visualize the studio layout and the protagonist's position within it. The contrast between street dance fluidity and K-pop precision is well-executed through specific technical details. The protagonist's muscle memory betrayals feel authentic—the "wrist cocked at a street-dancer's angle," the tendency to "ride the beat" versus being caged by rhythm, and the instinctive deeper body rolls all effectively illustrate the cultural and stylistic collision. The use of dance terminology feels natural rather than forced. The first-person voice maintains consistency throughout, capturing the protagonist's cultural displacement and class anxiety without becoming overwrought. The internal monologue effectively reveals the character's struggle between conformity and authenticity. The countdown clock creates effective tension and pacing structure. However, the piece suffers from some predictable elements. The harsh instructor who sees potential, the moment of rebellion followed by grudging acceptance, and the final compromise solution follow familiar narrative beats. The ending, while not entirely unearned, resolves perhaps too neatly with the instructor's sudden shift to encouraging the protagonist to "bend" rather than break formation. The dialogue, while limited, serves its purpose effectively. Min's clipped commands and the protagonist's whispered response feel authentic to the power dynamic and cultural context. The prose generally flows well, though some passages lean toward the mechanical in their precision—which may be intentional given the subject matter but occasionally distances the reader from the emotional core. The emotional complexity is present but could be deeper. While the protagonist's anxiety and frustration come through clearly, the internal conflict between artistic integrity and survival could be more nuanced. The piece touches on class anxiety but doesn't fully explore its implications. The technical achievement in maintaining physical coherence throughout is impressive and rare in creative writing. The author successfully creates a vivid kinetic experience that fulfills the prompt's demanding requirements while telling a coherent story with genuine emotional stakes. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 14.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 15.0, Emotionally Complex: 13.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6.0, Unearned Transformations: 7.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Gothic-fantasy-humour: Court of the Dreaming — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
Court of the Dreaming: Territorial Dream Dispute. Write a dark fantasy scene with comedic elements set in the Courts of Dream where a case is being heard to determine ownership of a contested dreamscape. Mora, the Slavic nightmare spirit who sits on sleepers' chests causing paralysis and terror, has recently invaded and subverted a carefully crafted recurring dream of a schoolboy, Max. The dream originally featured the boy lying in a field of wildflowers with his crush, always culminating in a muddled almost-kiss and a pleasant confusion of emotions. This dream was the meticulous work of "First Love" (a rosy-cheeked, slightly bashful entity who crafts formative romantic experiences). However, Mora has recently infiltrated the dreamscape, transforming the scene so that when the girl playfully mounts the boy, she morphs into a terrifying yet somehow still confusingly attractive vision who proceeds to sit on his chest and feed on his breath. Mora's defense largely consists of the practical argument that "a girl's gotta eat too, you know?" Write this scene as it unfolds in the courtroom of the Dreaming, with Dream's demeanour, typically impenetrable, betraying a sense of irritation at having to adjudicate such matters. The comedy should derive from the farcical situation, familiar elements of courtroom drama intersecting with the fantastical world of the Dreaming, and the droll portrayal of the characters and their interactions. The humor should be dark and understated throughout. Dream is tempted to some mischief in the resolution of the case. Use descriptives sparingly when setting scene. Write for readability & engagement. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Court of the Dreaming sits in a space that neither glows nor dims, its vaulted, ribbed ceiling vanishing into a haze of mist and half-remembered lullabies. Columns wind upward like the trunks of trees that have forgotten their roots, and the gallery is crowded with the usual suspects: a murder of crows in ill-fitting wigs; a jury of somnambulists, heads lolling; and, in the shadows, a trio of minor nightmares bickering over a popcorn kernel. At the bench sits Dream, pale and sharp-eyed, his robes suggesting both midnight and the memory of starlight. His fingers drum, just audibly, against the arm of his throne, creating a rhythm that’s almost—almost—comforting. The mood is not improved by the defendant’s intermittent cackling. Before him, the plaintive: First Love, a blushing figure in a cardigan two sizes too large, clutching a bouquet of wildflowers that, if one looked closely, murmured encouragements in voices of adolescent hope. On the other side: Mora, the nightmare. She hunches forward with the languor of someone who’s recently dined well, her eyes glittering, her lips oddly red. Shadows cling to her like perfume. Every few moments, she emits a giggle that ricochets through the rafters, unsettling the crows and causing the faintest twitch at the corner of Dream’s mouth. Dream clears his throat. It is the sort of sound that could end an empire or, in this case, silence a courtroom. “Let us begin. The case of Max, recurring dreamer, age twelve, versus Mora, eater of breath, invader of dreams. First Love, you may present your grievance.” First Love steps forward, cheeks aflame, voice trembling like a first confession. “Your Lordship, I—I have carefully nurtured young Max’s recurring dream these past six months. It is a work of great delicacy—a field of wildflowers, a gentle summer dusk, and the sweet ache of almost, but not quite, touching lips. My intention: to foster the subtle confusion and exquisite hope of first affection. The, um, muddled almost-kiss is essential. It is, if I may say, practically curriculum.” He glances at the gallery for support, but the crows only preen. “Recently, said dream has been—” he fumbles for a word, “—hijacked, perverted, and rendered…nightmarish. Instead of innocence, there is terror. Instead of soft longing, there is—” he shudders, “—breathlessness of a very different sort.” Dream fixes Mora with a gaze that could put the moon out. “Mora. Your defense?” Mora leans forward, her hair sliding like oil over her shoulders. “A girl’s gotta eat too, you know?” she drawls, her accent thick with the promise of distant thunder. “I saw a tasty morsel—such lovely lungs, so much potential for exquisite panic. And, if I may, I left the confusion. I only deepened it.” She grins, sharp-toothed. “Besides, what is first love if not a little terrifying?” A ripple of laughter—nervous, involuntary—passes through the gallery. One of the crows coughs. First Love, scandalized, tightens his grip on the flowers. “He awoke bathed in sweat! He has begun to fear the field. He flinched when his crush touched his arm in waking life. He is now writing poetry about the futility of existence. Bad poetry.” Mora shrugs, unconcerned. “Artistic growth. You’re welcome.” Dream sighs, the sound like the wind through the bones of ancient trees. “Let us consult the evidence.” A shimmering tableau appears in the air. There is Max, lying in wildflowers with the girl, face flushed, her hair haloed in light. They lean in; the world swims. Then: the girl’s eyes blacken, her smile widens, she straddles his chest, and suddenly, Max gasps, paralyzed, as Mora’s shadowy form feeds upon his dreams. The gallery murmurs in appreciation. The crows clap, a sound like wet branches snapping. First Love points accusingly. “This is not formative! This is… de-formative!” Mora licks her lips, sighing contentedly. “I gave him a memory he’ll never forget. He’ll never trust a summer dusk again. That’s formative, darling.” Dream pinches the bridge of his nose, an entirely mortal gesture. “This court is not in the business of adjudicating taste. The question is one of ownership. Does the recurring dream belong to its original architect—” he nods at First Love, “—or is it fair game for predation?” A somnambulist juror snores. Another jerks awake, muttering, “Objection, relevance,” before slumping again. Dream’s eyes flicker with a mischief he normally suppresses. He leans forward, voice silken. “Mora, if I were to rule in your favor, would you content yourself with this one meal, or would you make a habit of gorging on young love’s labor?” Mora grins. “Who could ever be satisfied with just one taste?” First Love looks on the verge of tears. “Please, Lord Dream. There are so few places left for innocence.” Dream considers. The silence stretches, wraps itself around the room like a winding sheet. Finally, he speaks. “I propose a compromise.” His smile is thin as a crescent moon. “The dream shall be divided. On alternate nights, Max will experience First Love’s meadow, complete with confusion and hope. On the other nights, Mora may visit, but—” he raises a finger, “—she must confine herself to the role of an unsettlingly attractive classmate, no chest-sitting, no breath-feeding, only the usual adolescent torments: ambiguous glances, cryptic text messages, and the occasional dream embarrassment.” Mora scowls. “No feeding?” Dream’s smile never wavers. “You may feed, but only on the ambient anxiety of adolescence, not the breath. There is, I assure you, an inexhaustible supply.” The gallery nods, the crows mutter, “Hear, hear.” First Love sags with relief, clutching his flowers to his chest. Dream bangs his gavel, which sounds suspiciously like a heartbeat. “So ruled. Court adjourned.” As Mora slinks out, she cackles, softer now, “See you in algebra, Max.” First Love bows, blushing furiously, and the crows rise in a raucous flurry, already debating the next case: the matter of the endlessly falling elevator and its disputed architect. Dream remains, alone on his throne, the faintest smile playing across his lips, savoring the knowledge that for Max—and perhaps for all dreamers—the line between first love and first terror will remain deliciously unclear.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a dark fantasy courtroom scene with comedic elements set in the Courts of Dream. The author captures the essence of Neil Gaiman's Sandman universe effectively, particularly in the portrayal of Dream's character - aloof, irritated by mundane matters, yet capable of subtle mischief. The worldbuilding is economical but evocative, establishing the surreal courtroom atmosphere without excessive description. The gallery populated with "crows in ill-fitting wigs" and "somnambulists" as jurors creates the right blend of absurd and ominous. The author wisely follows the instruction to use descriptives sparingly while still creating a vivid sense of place. Character voices are well-differentiated and consistent. Dream's formal, slightly archaic speech patterns feel authentic, while Mora's casual dismissiveness ("A girl's gotta eat too, you know?") provides effective contrast. First Love's nervous, stammering delivery captures the entity's nature perfectly. The dialogue drives the scene forward while revealing character motivations naturally. The humor works on multiple levels - from the absurdity of the situation to specific comedic beats like the somnambulist juror's mistimed "Objection, relevance." The dark comedy emerges organically from the premise rather than feeling forced. The line about Max "writing poetry about the futility of existence. Bad poetry" is particularly effective. The resolution demonstrates Dream's characteristic cunning while serving the story's thematic concerns. The compromise feels both practical and mischievous, true to Dream's nature of finding solutions that satisfy no one completely while serving his own amusement. The prose maintains good flow and readability throughout. Sentences vary in length and structure, creating natural rhythm. The author avoids purple prose while still crafting memorable images and turns of phrase. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the comedy could be sharper, and the ending, while satisfying, doesn't quite reach the heights of brilliance the setup promises. The piece also could have pushed the dark comedy elements slightly further to achieve maximum impact. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
